text:
What does your wealth shape now? what can it purchase now?
What is your standing worth now?
What are your doctrines, your schooling, your customs, your laws, now?
Where are your taunts of existence now?
Where are your doubts about the spirit now?
source_text:
What is your money-making now? what can it do now?
What is your respectability now?
What are your theology, tuition, society, traditions, statute-books, now?
Where are your jibes of being now?
Where are your cavils about the soul now?

text:
A wail drifts in, unbroken, toward my ear—  
the voice of the one I love, pitched close, half-frightened, unsure.  
The tide I’m bound to ride, tell me now—  
tell me what shore I’m racing toward, name the port I’m after.
source_text:
Despairing cries float ceaselessly toward me,
The call of my nearest lover, putting forth, alarm’d, uncertain,
The sea I am quickly to sail, come tell me,
Come tell me where I am speeding, tell me my destination.

text:
Though famine stretch across the land,
I’d never miss one grain,
Such wealth adorns my humble spread,
My stores shine without stain.
I wonder what the wealthy know—
A merchant prince, a lord?
I think that with a single seed
I reign above them all.
source_text:
It might be famine all around,
I could not miss an ear,
Such plenty smiles upon my board,
My garner shows so fair.
I wonder how the rich may feel, --
An Indiaman -- an Earl?
I deem that I with but a crumb
Am sovereign of them all.

text:
I make no claim to know—this life  
Of missteps, blindness, endless strife,  
Where nothing stands, all things just seem,  
And we are shadows of the dream.
source_text:
I dare not guess; but in this life
Of error, ignorance, and strife,
Where nothing is, but all things seem,
And we the shadows of the dream,

text:
I shall be your devoted priest and raise a shrine
In some secluded corner of my soul,
Where thoughts like boughs, with sweet and aching twine,
Not pines, will whisper as the breezes roll:
Far, far away those shadowed groves will spread
To cloak the rugged slopes with feathered green;
And there by streams, soft winds, and bees that tread,
The mossy Dryads will in slumber lean;
And at the heart of this serene domain
A blushing holy place I will sustain
With woven trellises of active mind,
With blooms, and chimes, and unnamed stars above,
With every charm that Fancy's art can find,
Who tends her flowers yet never twice the same:
And there for you all tender joys will dwell
That dusky thought can spin,
A gleaming lamp, a window wide as well,
To welcome warm Love in!
source_text:
Yes, I will be thy priest, and build a fane
In some untrodden region of my mind,
Where branched thoughts, new grown with pleasant pain,
Instead of pines shall murmur in the wind:
Far, far around shall those dark-cluster'd trees
Fledge the wild-ridged mountains steep by steep;
And there by zephyrs, streams, and birds, and bees,
The moss-lain Dryads shall be lull'd to sleep;
And in the midst of this wide quietness
A rosy sanctuary will I dress
With the wreath'd trellis of a working brain,
With buds, and bells, and stars without a name,
With all the gardener Fancy e'er could feign,
Who breeding flowers, will never breed the same:
And there shall be for thee all soft delight
That shadowy thought can win,
A bright torch, and a casement ope at night,
To let the warm Love in!

text:
I love to wake on a morning in June,  
When birds call out from each branch they adorn;  
Far off the hunter blows his tune,  
And the skylark joins me in the dawn:  
Oh, what dear company is born!
source_text:
I love to rise in a summer morn,
When the birds sing on every tree;
The distant huntsman winds his horn,
And the skylark sings with me:
O what sweet company!

text:
A curse now haunts your homeland, plain to see—
Truth beaten down, all justice bought and sold,
The earth’s old boundaries torn up greedily,
And mountains raised of fraudulent, cold gold
That roar like storms before the throne of doom.
source_text:
Thy country’s curse is on thee! Justice sold,
Truth trampled, Nature’s landmarks overthrown,
And heaps of fraud-accumulated gold,
Plead, loud as thunder, at Destruction’s throne.

text:
Just three guns still answer—
one, the captain’s own, aimed hard at the flagship’s spine,
two, thick with grapeshot and canister, sweeping the rifle-fire quiet,
scouring the deck clean.
source_text:
Only three guns are in use,
One is directed by the captain himself against the enemy’s main-mast,
Two well serv’d with grape and canister silence his musketry and
clear his decks.

text:
In twilight hush I wait; and often before  
I've nearly adored calm, unhurried Death,  
Coaxed him with tender words in verse I bore,  
To draw my silent breathing from its depth;  
Now death feels richer than it ever could,  
To end at midnight free of any ache,  
While you release your spirit to the wood  
In rapturous break!  
You'd still sing on, though I hear not a strain—  
Turned to mere earth beneath your high refrain.
source_text:
Darkling I listen; and, for many a time
I have been half in love with easeful Death,
Call'd him soft names in many a mused rhyme,
To take into the air my quiet breath;
Now more than ever seems it rich to die,
To cease upon the midnight with no pain,
While thou art pouring forth thy soul abroad
In such an ecstasy!
Still wouldst thou sing, and I have ears in vain--
To thy high requiem become a sod.

text:
For their sake, the pearl diver stilled his lungs,
And plunged unclothed toward the ravenous shark;
For them his ears streamed crimson; for them, stung,
The seal upon the frozen floe let mark
Its dying cry, pierced through; for them were wrung
A thousand souls in anguish deep and stark:
Unknowing half, they spun a simple wheel
That drove keen tortures, flaying without heal.
source_text:
For them the Ceylon diver held his breath,
And went all naked to the hungry shark;
For them his ears gush'd blood; for them in death
The seal on the cold ice with piteous bark
Lay full of darts; for them alone did seethe
A thousand men in troubles wide and dark:
Half-ignorant, they turn'd an easy wheel,
That set sharp racks at work, to pinch and peel.

text:
Once I was bound like a young calf,
For genteel cursing and drinking;
Once I was scolded in church's behalf,
For tumbling a lass while a-winking.
source_text:
I ance was tied up like a stirk,
For civilly swearing and quaffin;
I ance was abus’d i’ the kirk,
For towsing a lass i’ my daffin.

text:
I walked as though caught in a blissful waking dream:
Those blood-stained ranks, so newly brought to peace,
Were, step by step, through sorrow’s healing stream,
From rage to tenderness given sweet release,
And each on each now smiled with shy increase,
Because of wrongs they’d done—the gentle weight
Of such kind glances coaxed their hearts to cease,
And drew them with a slow, magnetic gait
Toward loving freedom’s just and even state.
source_text:
I trod as one tranced in some rapturous vision:
Those bloody bands so lately reconciled,
Were, ever as they went, by the contrition
Of anger turned to love, from ill beguiled,
And every one on them more gently smiled,
Because they had done evil:—the sweet awe
Of such mild looks made their own hearts grow mild,
And did with soft attraction ever draw
Their spirits to the love of freedom’s equal law.

text:
Flame, that from light yields only haze,
Let him glimpse past the deepest floor
Of truth’s lucent spring—while you and I, love,
Descend, as we must, to earth’s embrace,
We might learn what he never saw.
source_text:
Fire, which ex luce praebens fumum,
Made him beyond the bottom see
Of truth’s clear well—when I and you, Ma’am,
Go, as we shall do, subter humum,
We may know more than he.

text:
My verse, worn out from countless lays
On robe, and sash, and sober greys,
Now trembles faint at her own ways,
Fearing their scorn,
Lest they unleash their sacred blaze
And damn her song.
source_text:
My musie, tir’d wi’ mony a sonnet
On gown, an’ ban’, an’ douse black bonnet,
Is grown right eerie now she’s done it,
Lest they should blame her,
An’ rouse their holy thunder on it
An anathem her.

text:
Watch for the stream when March arrives,
As the rivers crest and spill,
And snow from the ridges comes rushing down,
While bridges break at will.
source_text:
Then look out for the little brook in March,
When the rivers overflow,
And the snows come hurrying from the hills,
And the bridges often go.

text:
Refrain—Do you know who dwells in that town,
Bathed in the glow of the setting sun?
The sweetest girl in that town,
Lit by the light of the dying sun.
source_text:
Chorus—O wat ye wha’s in yon town,
Ye see the e’enin sun upon,
The dearest maid’s in yon town,
That e’ening sun is shining on.

text:
Should the dead press through on days like these—
across the sun-struck fields, the long roads unwinding,
the wagons heaped high, the barns brimming with light?
source_text:
But on these days of brightness,
On the far-stretching beauteous landscape, the roads and lanes the
high-piled farm-wagons, and the fruits and barns,
Should the dead intrude?

text:
Someone held out a bloom to me,
More radiant than spring ever grew;
But I said, “I’ve a lovely rose tree,”
And I turned from that exquisite hue.
source_text:
A flower was offered to me,
Such a flower as May never bore;
But I said, ‘I’ve a pretty rose tree,’
And I passed the sweet flower o’er.

text:
Can I witness another's ache  
And not feel that same pain wake?  
Can I watch a stranger's despair  
And not hunt for gentle care?
source_text:
Can I see another’s woe,
And not be in sorrow too?
Can I see another’s grief,
And not seek for kind relief?

text:
That agèd Beadsman caught the gentle strain;
And thus it fell, since many doors stood free,
Through bustling haste. Anon, on high, a train
Of silver, growling trumpets scolded keen:
The tiered rooms, prepared in splendid sheen,
Were gleaming for a thousand guests to hold:
The sculpted angels, watchful and serene,
Gazed, where the cornice crowns their heads of gold,
With tresses swept behind, and wings across their bosoms fold.
source_text:
That ancient Beadsman heard the prelude soft;
And so it chanc'd, for many a door was wide,
From hurry to and fro. Soon, up aloft,
The silver, snarling trumpets 'gan to chide:
The level chambers, ready with their pride,
Were glowing to receive a thousand guests:
The carved angels, ever eager-eyed,
Star'd, where upon their heads the cornice rests,
With hair blown back, and wings put cross-wise on their breasts.

text:
A buxom, cheerful matron steps inside
And settles near the flame,
Then brings her cheese and blade with pride;
The maidens hide their shame:
The elder men, about the prayer,
From end to end they sway;
Till one removes his cap with care,
And leads them, like a rein,
All through that day.
source_text:
In comes a gawsie, gash guidwife,
An’ sits down by the fire,
Syne draws her kebbuck an’ her knife;
The lasses they are shyer:
The auld guidmen, about the grace
Frae side to side they bother;
Till some ane by his bonnet lays,
An’ gies them’t like a tether,
Fu’ lang that day.

text:
We launch at last, we drift,
Through Time and Space, O soul, made ready now,
Whole and fully armed, (O bliss! O harvest of it all!) to meet them, O soul.
source_text:
Then we burst forth, we float,
In Time and Space O soul, prepared for them,
Equal, equipt at last, (O joy! O fruit of all!) them to fulfil O soul.

text:
Their rooster’s crows and strange guffaws
Set both our ears on edge, you see;
The famished bike would scrape and fret,
Till we were worn and woeful, we:
But a kingly ghost, once flung aside,
A captive, eighteen years astray,
He fired a Fiddler in the North,
Who knocked them heels o’er head that day.
source_text:
Their capon craws an’ queer “ha, ha’s,”
They made our lugs grow eerie, O;
The hungry bike did scrape and fyke,
Till we were wae and weary, O:
But a royal ghaist, wha ance was cas’d,
A prisoner, aughteen year awa’,
He fir’d a Fiddler in the North,
That dang them tapsalteerie, O.

text:
When first I reached the Stewart Kyle,
My thoughts were far from steady;
Wherever I would walk or ride,
A sweetheart still was ready.
source_text:
When first I came to Stewart Kyle,
My mind it was na steady;
Where’er I gaed, where’er I rade,
A mistress still I had aye.

text:
Then from his Lordship I’ll take heed,
To greet with calm and equal speed
All stations, high or lowly;
No upright, decent soul should dread
To face young Daer, nobly bred,
For he but meets kin solely.
source_text:
Then from his Lordship I shall learn,
Henceforth to meet with unconcern
One rank as weel’s another;
Nae honest, worthy man need care
To meet with noble youthful Daer,
For he but meets a brother.

text:
Fierce in my breast the loyal currents beat,
I gazed on Scotia’s laurelled crest displayed:
Her stately shape, in sorrow bowed and sweet,
The fire within her gaze by tears arrayed.
source_text:
Wild to my heart the filial pulses glow,
’Twas Caledonia’s trophied shield I view’d:
Her form majestic droop’d in pensive woe,
The lightning of her eye in tears imbued.

text:
Behold, she rises, borne on endless time,
A living throne above the fleeting years.
God keep the Queen!
Unnumbered multitudes attend her reign,
Steadfast and swift, with joy they bear her train,
Her sovereign grace their triumph and refrain.
God keep the Queen!
source_text:
See, she comes throned on high,
On swift Eternity!
God save the Queen!
Millions on millions wait,
Firm, rapid, and elate,
On her majestic state!
God save the Queen!

text:
The daisy tracks the sun’s slow arc,
and when his blazing stroll grows dark,
she nestles at his tread.
At dawn he sees her waiting there.
“Why, little thief, do you draw near?”
“For love is sweet,” she said.
source_text:
The daisy follows soft the sun,
And when his golden walk is done,
Sits shyly at his feet.
He, waking, finds the flower near.
"Wherefore, marauder, art thou here?"
"Because, sir, love is sweet!"

text:
From Tweed’s far banks to Spey we’d stray,
And passed our days in fine array;
No Lowland look could make him run—
My bold, brave lad, John Highlandman.
Sing ho, &c.
source_text:
We ranged a’ from Tweed to Spey,
An’ liv’d like lords an’ ladies gay;
For a Lalland face he feared none,—
My gallant, braw John Highlandman.
Sing hey, &c.

text:
Strip back your layers—you’re not tainted to me, not worn or cast off.
I see past the wool and the calico, no matter the weave,
And I circle, close, hungry, unflagging—nothing can shake me loose.
source_text:
Undrape! you are not guilty to me, nor stale nor discarded,
I see through the broadcloth and gingham whether or no,
And am around, tenacious, acquisitive, tireless, and cannot be shaken away.

text:
The herd-path cuts the dark dust clean and plain,
As though the beasts were turning from this shore
Back toward their origin—that ghostly plain
Of asphodel where my own cattle graze before.
But his trail baffles me; I try in vain
To find the language for what I explore—
Those prints suggest he crossed the dunes unplanned,
Not on his feet, and not upon his hands.
source_text:
‘The cattle’s track on the black dust, full well
Is evident, as if they went towards
The place from which they came—that asphodel
Meadow, in which I feed my many herds,—
HIS steps were most incomprehensible—
I know not how I can describe in words
Those tracks—he could have gone along the sands
Neither upon his feet nor on his hands;—

text:
You cannot fool me with your silence—
I’ve seen how all the natural world gives way
before the one who’s ready. Skies and trees,
though wordless, catch his voice—and you, O sun,
with all your wrenchings, your upheavals, sudden ruptures,
your vast flaring tongues,
I read them clearly. I know those fires,
those tremors, from inside.
source_text:
(Thou canst not with thy dumbness me deceive,
I know before the fitting man all Nature yields,
Though answering not in words, the skies, trees, hear his voice--and
thou O sun,
As for thy throes, thy perturbations, sudden breaks and shafts of
flame gigantic,
I understand them, I know those flames, those perturbations well.)

text:
When the stars let fall their lances bright,
And drenched the sky with sorrow's light,
Did He look upon His work with delight?
Did He who shaped the lamb shape you?
source_text:
When the stars threw down their spears,
And watered heaven with their tears,
Did He smile His work to see?
Did He who made the lamb make thee?

text:
From the vast hollow
of joy and marvel,
whose hollows are halls of prismed glass;
from those cloud-built spires
where crowned minds keep their fires
and gaze on your turning, you radiant Hours!
source_text:
From that deep abyss
Of wonder and bliss,
Whose caverns are crystal palaces;
From those skiey towers
Where Thought’s crowned powers
Sit watching your dance, ye happy Hours!

text:
My glories are a carnival of beasts,
Yet their unrivaled display
Will charm the ages still to come
When I have passed away,
A mound in unremembered weeds,
Known only to the clover and the day.
source_text:
My splendors are menagerie;
But their competeless show
Will entertain the centuries
When I am, long ago,
An island in dishonored grass,
Whom none but daisies know.

text:
These cravings to keep them—exhausted by endless fervor,
wrenched by the battle inside—
These hungers forever petitioning, surging in pleas from my chest,
While still in my asking I cling to my city,
Day after day, year upon year O city, pacing your avenues,
Where you hold me bound for a season, refusing to release me,
Yet feeding me full, enriching my spirit, you grant me forever faces;
(O I behold what I fled from, turning against my own prayers,
I see my soul trampling the very thing it demanded.)
source_text:
These demanding to have them, (tired with ceaseless excitement, and
rack’d by the war-strife,)
These to procure incessantly asking, rising in cries from my heart,
While yet incessantly asking still I adhere to my city,
Day upon day and year upon year O city, walking your streets,
Where you hold me enchain’d a certain time refusing to give me up,
Yet giving to make me glutted, enrich’d of soul, you give me forever faces;
(O I see what I sought to escape, confronting, reversing my cries,
see my own soul trampling down what it ask’d for.)

text:
Let him move fast—the prey slips by,
a dish already cold.
Rage, once it’s gorged, drops lifeless down;
it’s hunger makes it swell.
source_text:
Let him be quick, the viand flits,
It is a faded meat.
Anger as soon as fed is dead;
'T is starving makes it fat.

text:
She passed—this was how she passed away;  
And when her final breath had gone,  
She gathered up her humble clothes  
And set out toward the sun.
source_text:
She died, -- this was the way she died;
And when her breath was done,
Took up her simple wardrobe
And started for the sun.

text:
The wind began to sway the grass  
With dark and rumbling sound —  
It hurled a threat against the ground,  
A threat against the clouds.
source_text:
The wind begun to rock the grass
With threatening tunes and low, --
He flung a menace at the earth,
A menace at the sky.

text:
I won’t be cowed by the senseless,
I’ll find the sting they aim at me and name it,
I’ll make empires and bright streets bow down,
This is what America taught me—the sheer weight of it, and I pass it on.
source_text:
I will not be outfaced by irrational things,
I will penetrate what it is in them that is sarcastic upon me,
I will make cities and civilizations defer to me,
This is what I have learnt from America--it is the amount, and it I
teach again.

text:
From the severed limb, the lost hand’s absence,
I loosen the crusted gauze, lift the dead tissue, rinse the wound clean,
The soldier sinks back to his pillow, neck arced, head tilting aside,
Eyelids sealed, skin gone pale, he cannot face the raw end of bone,
Has not yet let his gaze find it.
source_text:
From the stump of the arm, the amputated hand,
I undo the clotted lint, remove the slough, wash off the matter and blood,
Back on his pillow the soldier bends with curv’d neck and side falling head,
His eyes are closed, his face is pale, he dares not look on the
bloody stump,
And has not yet look’d on it.

text:
Now Nature garbs the blooming field,
And all is fresh and soft as thee,
O wilt thou walk its paths wi’ me,
And swear thou’lt be my Dearie, O.
Lassie wi’ the, &c.
source_text:
Now Nature cleeds the flowery lea,
And a’ is young and sweet like thee,
O wilt thou share its joys wi’ me,
And say thou’lt be my Dearie, O.
Lassie wi’ the, &c.

text:
Splendid, each thing—
splendid, this sweep we call space, home to uncounted presences,
splendid the secret of movement in all that lives, down to the least midge,
splendid the gift of language, the senses, the flesh,
splendid the fading light—splendid the thin gleam caught
on the new moon hung low in the west,
splendid all I witness or brush against or hear, down to the final mote.
source_text:
Illustrious every one!
Illustrious what we name space, sphere of unnumber’d spirits,
Illustrious the mystery of motion in all beings, even the tiniest insect,
Illustrious the attribute of speech, the senses, the body,
Illustrious the passing light--illustrious the pale reflection on
the new moon in the western sky,
Illustrious whatever I see or hear or touch, to the last.

text:
Can I watch a teardrop descend
And not sense my grief in its bend?
Can a father see his own
Cry, and not be overthrown?
source_text:
Can I see a falling tear,
And not feel my sorrow’s share?
Can a father see his child
Weep, nor be with sorrow filled?

text:
No, no, let’s keep playing—the light hasn’t gone,
And sleep is still far from our reach;
Look, the birds trace their paths across the sun,
And the hills wear a blanket of sheep.
source_text:
‘No, no, let us play, for it is yet day,
And we cannot go to sleep;
Besides, in the sky the little birds fly,
And the hills are all covered with sheep.’

text:
I catch the music of this land, its many voices rising clear—
the mechanic’s tune, light and steady as it ought to be,
the carpenter’s hum measuring the plank’s true edge,
the mason’s low chant as he sets stone, or rests at dusk,
the boatman’s call across the water, the deckhand’s
song drifting up from the steamboat’s rail,
the shoemaker’s melody bent over his bench, the hatter’s
soft refrain standing at his block,
the wood-cutter’s rhythm, the ploughboy’s morning hymn,
his noon pause, his sundown hush,
the warm singing of the mother, the young wife at her tasks,
the girl at her sewing or her wash,
each voice claiming only what is its own and no other’s,
the day holding what the day holds—then at night the gathering
of young men, sturdy and easy with each other,
singing full-throated, their strong and joyful songs.
source_text:
I hear America singing, the varied carols I hear,
Those of mechanics, each one singing his as it should be blithe and strong,
The carpenter singing his as he measures his plank or beam,
The mason singing his as he makes ready for work, or leaves off work,
The boatman singing what belongs to him in his boat, the deckhand
singing on the steamboat deck,
The shoemaker singing as he sits on his bench, the hatter singing as
he stands,
The wood-cutter’s song, the ploughboy’s on his way in the morning,
or at noon intermission or at sundown,
The delicious singing of the mother, or of the young wife at work,
or of the girl sewing or washing,
Each singing what belongs to him or her and to none else,
The day what belongs to the day--at night the party of young
fellows, robust, friendly,
Singing with open mouths their strong melodious songs.

text:
I walked out to my lovely flowering bush,  
To care for her morning and night;  
But she turned aside, stung by envy’s push,  
And her prickles were all my delight.
source_text:
Then I went to my pretty rose tree,
To tend her by day and by night;
But my rose turned away with jealousy,
And her thorns were my only delight.

text:
When tempers flare between two friends,
And stubborn pride won’t meet or bend,
How swift a dram of grain can mend
The rift they carry!
The poorest purse will find its end
Inside the barley.
source_text:
When neibors anger at a plea,
An’ just as wud as wud can be,
How easy can the barley brie
Cement the quarrel!
It’s aye the cheapest lawyer’s fee,
To taste the barrel.

text:
No! those times have slipped from view,
Their moments faded, worn, and blue,
And their seconds lie interred
Beneath the trampled, heavy shroud
Cast by countless seasons’ leaves:
So often have the frost’s sharp shears,
The bitter North, the biting East,
Howled wild storms to celebrate
The woodland’s rustling, woolen cloak,
Since none knew deeds or landlord’s yoke.
source_text:
No! those days are gone away,
And their hours are old and gray,
And their minutes buried all
Under the down-trodden pall
Of the leaves of many years:
Many times have winter's shears,
Frozen North, and chilling East,
Sounded tempests to the feast
Of the forest's whispering fleeces,
Since men knew nor rent nor leases.

text:
I pause at midday on the crown of the sky,  
Then, step by heavy step, descend into  
The Atlantic’s dusk, a slowly darkening sigh;  
Because I leave, the clouds in somber hue  
Grieve and gather—yet no gaze could be as mild  
As mine that calms them from the farthest western isle.
source_text:
I stand at noon upon the peak of Heaven,
Then with unwilling steps I wander down
Into the clouds of the Atlantic even;
For grief that I depart they weep and frown:
What look is more delightful than the smile
With which I soothe them from the western isle?

text:
At dawn the skylark stirs from her bed,
And climbs through the sky with dew on her breast,
With the cheerful plowman she’ll carol and cry,
And at dusk she’ll glide home to her nest in the rye.
source_text:
The lav’rock in the morning she’ll rise frae her nest,
And mount i’ the air wi’ the dew on her breast,
And wi’ the merry ploughman she’ll whistle and sing,
And at night she’ll return to her nest back again.

text:
O blessed birds, whose songs surround her now,
And greet the year in blossom as it grows;
Let Spring receive a twofold welcome vow,
The time that brings my darling Jeanie close.
O know ye whose, &c.
source_text:
How blest ye birds that round her sing,
And welcome in the blooming year;
And doubly welcome be the Spring,
The season to my Jeanie dear.
O wat ye wha’s, &c.

text:
I should have been too sheltered, I know—
Too spared; dread too faint to show
That I could shape the cry
I had by heart just yesterday—
That searing plea, “Sabachthani,”
Spoken so fluidly here.
source_text:
I should have been too saved, I see,
Too rescued; fear too dim to me
That I could spell the prayer
I knew so perfect yesterday, --
That scalding one, "Sabachthani,"
Recited fluent here.

text:
Through me, the silenced cadences—
desires and bodies, their hidden inflections—
I draw back the curtain, let them stand in light,
indecencies made lucent, remade whole.
source_text:
Through me forbidden voices,
Voices of sexes and lusts, voices veil’d and I remove the veil,
Voices indecent by me clarified and transfigur’d.

text:
The dark-crowned eagle,  
With senses as sharp as a beagle,  
Swept over the peaks and the hollows,  
Through the slopes of Gemappe,  
He stumbled into a snare,  
Now let him escape as he may, may, may,  
Now let him escape as he may.
source_text:
The black-headed eagle,
As keen as a beagle,
He hunted o’er height and o’er howe,
In the braes o’ Gemappe,
He fell in a trap,
E’en let him come out as he dow, dow, dow,
E’en let him come out as he dow.

text:
The darkling dusk now closes swift,  
A fickle gale gives savage drift,  
That sullen cloud is thick with storm,  
Across the fields it takes its form;  
The chase is done, the moor forsaken,  
The hidden flocks no more are shaken;  
While I, with burdened heart, repair  
By Ayr’s forsaken verges there.
source_text:
The gloomy night is gath’ring fast,
Loud roars the wild, inconstant blast,
Yon murky cloud is foul with rain,
I see it driving o’er the plain;
The hunter now has left the moor.
The scatt’red coveys meet secure;
While here I wander, prest with care,
Along the lonely banks of Ayr.

text:
At dusk they hammered, at dawn they shaped the wood,
Until our goodman found himself with shame imbued;
On every forehead she set a horn to brood,
And vows that there they must remain.
We’ll conceal the Cooper, &c.
source_text:
They cooper’d at e’en, they cooper’d at morn,
Till our gudeman has gotten the scorn;
On ilka brow she’s planted a horn,
And swears that there they sall stan’ O.
We’ll hide the Cooper, &c.

text:
We’re placed on earth for just a brief span,
To learn to carry love’s fierce light;
These dark bodies and this sun-scorched skin
Are only a mist, a cool, sheltering wood.
source_text:
‘And we are put on earth a little space,
That we may learn to bear the beams of love;
And these black bodies and this sunburnt face
Are but a cloud, and like a shady grove.

text:
St. Agnes’ Eve—so sharp a cold it brought!
The owl, though thickly plumed, felt the frost’s sting;
The hare crept shivering where the ice had caught,
And mute the sheep in fleeces huddling cling:
Stiff were the Beadsman’s hands upon the string
Of beads, and from his lips a misty sigh,
Like sacred smoke from some old censer’s swing,
Rose heavenward as if it could not die,
Beyond the Virgin’s image while his prayer went high.
source_text:
St. Agnes' Eve--Ah, bitter chill it was!
The owl, for all his feathers, was a-cold;
The hare limp'd trembling through the frozen grass,
And silent was the flock in woolly fold:
Numb were the Beadsman's fingers, while he told
His rosary, and while his frosted breath,
Like pious incense from a censer old,
Seem'd taking flight for heaven, without a death,
Past the sweet Virgin's picture, while his prayer he saith.

text:
He’ll whisper the pledge Peter once made,
and I, stunned by the grief he shows,
will lose the burning bead of pain
that sears me now, that sears me now.
source_text:
He will tell me what Peter promised,
And I, for wonder at his woe,
I shall forget the drop of anguish
That scalds me now, that scalds me now.

text:
She led me to her faerie cave,
Where tears and heavy sorrows poured,
And there I closed her fierce, fierce gaze
With kisses four.
source_text:
She took me to her elfin grot,
And there she wept and sigh'd full sore,
And there I shut her wild, wild eyes
With kisses four.

text:
They spoke of one instructor, fixed and timeless,
Even God, who—bound by the need
For law and transgression—stood arrayed
Against the human, both servant and scourge.
source_text:
9 15. The stanza contains ten lines—lines 4-7 as follows:
There was one teacher, and must ever be,
They said, even God, who, the necessity
Of rule and wrong had armed against mankind,
His slave and his avenger there to be;

text:
A night or so ago, the moon  
Was just a gilded curve of chin,  
And now she lifts her flawless face  
To gaze upon the earth again.
source_text:
The moon was but a chin of gold
A night or two ago,
And now she turns her perfect face
Upon the world below.

text:
He passed beneath a humble, bending arch,
His towering feather sweeping strands of thread,
And while she whispered, "Woe—alas—the march!"
He stood within a moonlit chamber, spread
With lattice-shadows, cold, and still as dead.
"Now speak to me of Madeline," he cried,
"O speak, good Angela, by the sacred stead
Where only hidden sisters may abide,
When pious hands St. Agnes' fleece are weaving side by side."
source_text:
He follow'd through a lowly arched way,
Brushing the cobwebs with his lofty plume,
And as she mutter'd "Well-a--well-a-day!"
He found him in a little moonlight room,
Pale, lattic'd, chill, and silent as a tomb.
"Now tell me where is Madeline," said he,
"O tell me, Angela, by the holy loom
Which none but secret sisterhood may see,
When they St. Agnes' wool are weaving piously."

text:
His whispered pleas ascend, this saintly, steadfast soul;
Then lifts his lantern, rising from the stone,
And turns again, so gaunt, unshod, and pale,
Down the long nave with measured steps alone:
The carven dead, encased on either throne,
Seem locked in chill, repentant iron bands:
Lords, maidens, bowed in silent orison,
He treads between; and his frail heart withstands
Hardly the thought of their sharp frost in steel and woven strands.
source_text:
His prayer he saith, this patient, holy man;
Then takes his lamp, and riseth from his knees,
And back returneth, meagre, barefoot, wan,
Along the chapel aisle by slow degrees:
The sculptur'd dead, on each side, seem to freeze,
Emprison'd in black, purgatorial rails:
Knights, ladies, praying in dumb orat'ries,
He passeth by; and his weak spirit fails
To think how they may ache in icy hoods and mails.

text:
And mushrooms, lichen, a festering of spores
breathed from the damp earth’s pores;
wan, meat-hued, as though the rotting dead
had quickened with a pulse and spread.
source_text:
And agarics, and fungi, with mildew and mould
Started like mist from the wet ground cold;
Pale, fleshy, as if the decaying dead
With a spirit of growth had been animated!

text:
It wasn’t death—I was on my feet,
While the dead stay flat in the ground;
It wasn’t night—the bells all rang
With tongues stretched out for noon.
source_text:
It was not death, for I stood up,
And all the dead lie down;
It was not night, for all the bells
Put out their tongues, for noon.

text:
Behold that bud, with morning moisture crowned,
Amid its kindred thorns so shyly placed;
How swift its blush and fragrance are uncrowned,
Once plucked and as a trinket worn to waste.
source_text:
See yonder rosebud, rich in dew,
Amang its native briers sae coy;
How sune it tines its scent and hue,
When pu’d and worn a common toy.

text:
Dear mother, dear mother, the chapel is chill,
But the tavern is hearty, and cheerful, and warm.
And I know where I’m welcomed and treated with care—
The welcome in heaven could never compare.

This doom is dealt to anguished grace,
That long with hunger, grief, and chase
By mortal arrogance or chase
To sorrow’s ledge;
Till stripped of every crutch but grace,
It, broken, plunge!
source_text:
Such fate to suffering worth is giv’n,
Who long with wants and woes has striv’n,
By human pride or cunning driv’n
To mis’ry’s brink;
Till wrench’d of ev’ry stay but Heav’n,
He, ruin’d, sink!

text:
Now here with you, soft bleating lamb,
I can lay myself to rest,
Or think of Him who shared your name,
Follow your grazing, and weep with the blessed.
For, bathed in life’s flowing stream,
My bright mane will gleam
Like gold without end,
As I watch the fold I defend.
source_text:
‘And now beside thee, bleating lamb,
I can lie down and sleep,
Or think on Him who bore thy name,
Graze after thee, and weep.
For, washed in life’s river,
My bright mane for ever
Shall shine like the gold,
As I guard o’er the fold.

text:
I held a coin of gold once—  
it slipped into the sand,  
and though the amount was modest  
and wealth lay on the land,  
it carried such a meaning  
to my careful, watchful eye  
that when it stayed unfound,  
I sank down just to sigh.
source_text:
I had a guinea golden;
I lost it in the sand,
And though the sum was simple,
And pounds were in the land,
Still had it such a value
Unto my frugal eye,
That when I could not find it
I sat me down to sigh.

text:
Now I know it is so, what I once only sensed—
What brushed me as I drifted through the grass,
What whispered when I lay still in the dark,
And again on the shore, beneath stars fading toward dawn.
source_text:
Space and Time! now I see it is true, what I guess’d at,
What I guess’d when I loaf’d on the grass,
What I guess’d while I lay alone in my bed,
And again as I walk’d the beach under the paling stars of the morning.

text:
The only spirit I have known  
Wore lace of mechlin thread;  
No shoe or sole was ever shown,  
Like snowdrift was his tread.  
His pace was mute as a bird in flight,  
Yet swift as a deer instead;  
His garments strange, inlaid with light,  
Or woven of mistletoe.
source_text:
The only ghost I ever saw
Was dressed in mechlin, -- so;
He wore no sandal on his foot,
And stepped like flakes of snow.
His gait was soundless, like the bird,
But rapid, like the roe;
His fashions quaint, mosaic,
Or, haply, mistletoe.

text:
In the gaze of unwitting infant faces,  
Or oddly within the shuttered dead,  
In the first crack of morning or the night’s star-fall,  
Like a thinning, tender membrane of sleep,  
Concealed yet staying.
source_text:
In looks of fair unconscious babes,
Or strangely in the coffin’d dead,
Or show of breaking dawn or stars by night,
As some dissolving delicate film of dreams,
Hiding yet lingering.

text:
Father, mother, if the early shoots are pinched,
And petals scattered by the wind;
If the soft green growth is torn
From its gladness in the rising light,
By grief’s rough hand and worry’s chill—
source_text:
O father and mother if buds are nipped,
And blossoms blown away;
And if the tender plants are stripped
Of their joy in the springing day,
By sorrow and care’s dismay,—

text:
I seized my strength within my palm
And strode against the earth;
My store was less than David’s claim,
Yet doubled was my nerve.
source_text:
I took my power in my hand.
And went against the world;
'T was not so much as David had,
But I was twice as bold.

text:
Within the hushed and whispering entryway,
He found his youthful follower. “It’s no slight breach,
Lycius,” he spoke, “for one uncalled to prey
Upon your company, and darkly reach
With unwanted self among the shining band
Of younger friends; yet I must do this wrong,
And ask your pardon.” Lycius, blushing, took the hand
And guided the elder past the inner throng;
With soothing phrases and a gracious face
Turning the cynic’s bitterness to grace.
source_text:
He met within the murmurous vestibule
His young disciple. "'Tis no common rule,
Lycius," said he, "for uninvited guest
To force himself upon you, and infest
With an unbidden presence the bright throng
Of younger friends; yet must I do this wrong,
And you forgive me." Lycius blush'd, and led
The old man through the inner doors broad-spread;
With reconciling words and courteous mien
Turning into sweet milk the sophist's spleen.

text:
Bound to fall short, yet boundless in the leap.
For each lone vessel that parades the coast,
How many brave and capsized spirits sleep
In fleets that long ago were lost.
source_text:
Finite to fail, but infinite to venture.
For the one ship that struts the shore
Many's the gallant, overwhelmed creature
Nodding in navies nevermore.

text:
I have carved my own way forward,  
I have chanted the flesh and the spirit, sung the clash and the calm,  
the hymns of living and dying,  
and the songs of beginning, and revealed how beginnings never end.
source_text:
I have press’d through in my own right,
I have sung the body and the soul, war and peace have I sung, and
the songs of life and death,
And the songs of birth, and shown that there are many births.

text:
Look back once more, sweet Eliza!
One gentle glance before we go;
Grieve for the heart that aches to love you,
Could you wound its trust with woe?
Look back once more, sweet Eliza!
If your soul cannot return the flame,
Then in mercy veil the verdict
Under friendship’s tender name.
source_text:
Turn again, thou fair Eliza!
Ae kind blink before we part;
Rue on thy despairing lover,
Can’st thou break his faithfu’ heart?
Turn again, thou fair Eliza!
If to love thy heart denies,
Oh, in pity hide the sentence
Under friendship’s kind disguise!

text:
That moment I moved—a rabid gale keening,
Nothing in view but the sky’s fractured gleam,
Overhead the boom of the thunder careening,
While under my breath slid the wind’s frozen stream.
source_text:
’Twas then that I started, the wild storm was howling,
Nought was seen, save the lightning that danced on the sky,
Above me the crash of the thunder was rolling,
And low, chilling murmurs the blast wafted by.—

text:
Some claim it’s the music of the stars!
Some claim that radiant company
Of lost women and men!
Some hold it’s duty in the realm
Where we, at last, with heaven-lit face,
Shall, God willing, know for certain!
source_text:
Some say it is the spheres at play!
Some say that bright majority
Of vanished dames and men!
Some think it service in the place
Where we, with late, celestial face,
Please God, shall ascertain!

text:
And as dusk drifted down from the boundless sky,
And the land lay in hush, and the breeze was a sigh,
And joy, though grown softer, sank richer and far,
And the mask of the light slipped from dreams left ajar.
source_text:
And when evening descended from Heaven above,
And the Earth was all rest, and the air was all love,
And delight, though less bright, was far more deep,
And the day’s veil fell from the world of sleep,

text:
It was a sacred Thursday, their pure faces washed,
Children paired in procession, in scarlet, azure, emerald:
Silver-haired wardens led them forward, rods pale as winter,
Until into St. Paul’s vast dome they streamed like the river’s tide.
source_text:
’Twas on a holy Thursday, their innocent faces clean,
The children walking two and two, in red, and blue, and green:
Grey-headed beadles walked before, with wands as white as snow,
Till into the high dome of Paul’s they like Thames waters flow.

text:
Her bright horn sounds across the drowsing fields, and spills
(Like shepherds guiding tender buds through open air)
A wash of scent and color over slope and hill:
source_text:
Her clarion o’er the dreaming earth, and fill
(Driving sweet buds like flocks to feed in air)
With living hues and odours plain and hill:

text:
The willow, the mournful willow, named for the way its boughs,
draped in slender leaves, sweep downward to the earth like falling tears.
It has forever been hallowed by forsaken or sorrowful hearts. See Othello, IV.
iii. 24 seq.
source_text:
l. 224. willow, the weeping willow, so-called because its branches
with their long leaves droop to the ground, like dropping tears. It has
always been sacred to deserted or unhappy lovers. Cf. Othello, IV.
iii. 24 seq.

text:
Pour out your songs—
alone now, songs of the dark,
songs of aching love, songs the dying sing,
songs beneath the slow, sallow, thinning moon—
beneath that moon as she sinks, nearly touching the water—
wild, ruined songs of no hope.
source_text:
Shake out carols!
Solitary here, the night’s carols!
Carols of lonesome love! death’s carols!
Carols under that lagging, yellow, waning moon!
O under that moon where she droops almost down into the sea!
O reckless despairing carols.

text:
Bare and pale now, all their packs cast away,
They float up on the mist and play with the breeze.
And the angel said to Tom, if you walk a kind path,
You’ll have heaven as your home, and never taste sorrow.
source_text:
Then naked and white, all their bags left behind,
They rise upon clouds, and sport in the wind:
And the angel told Tom, if he’d be a good boy,
He’d have God for his father, and never want joy.

text:
You click-tongued speaker, fleece-crowned traveler—  
you bodies held in title, leaking salt or iron.  
You faces of men wearing the endless, readable gaze of animals.  
You starved outlier, whom the smallest among them spurn  
for your flickering speech and your flickering inwardness.  
You shortened ones of ice and tundra—Kamchatkan, Greenlander, Sámi.  
You southern islander, bare, ochre-black, ash-skinned, lip jutting,  
bent low, hunting your mouthful.  
You Kaffir, Amazigh, Sudanese.  
You gaunt, rough, unlettered Bedouin.  
You fever-clouds thickening in Madras, Nanjing, Kabul, Cairo.  
You night-lost wanderer of Amazonia—you Patagonian—you Fijian.  
I do not hold others far above you, not at all.  
I speak no syllable against you, back there where you linger—  
you will step forward in your own hour and stand beside me.
source_text:
You Hottentot with clicking palate! you woolly-hair’d hordes!
You own’d persons dropping sweat-drops or blood-drops!
You human forms with the fathomless ever-impressive countenances of brutes!
You poor koboo whom the meanest of the rest look down upon for all
your glimmering language and spirituality!
You dwarf’d Kamtschatkan, Greenlander, Lapp!
You Austral negro, naked, red, sooty, with protrusive lip,
groveling, seeking your food!
You Caffre, Berber, Soudanese!
You haggard, uncouth, untutor’d Bedowee!
You plague-swarms in Madras, Nankin, Kaubul, Cairo!
You benighted roamer of Amazonia! you Patagonian! you Feejeeman!
I do not prefer others so very much before you either,
I do not say one word against you, away back there where you stand,
(You will come forward in due time to my side.)

text:
Let other bards stir wild commotion  
Of grapes and gods and drunken oceans,  
With bitter tales and harsh emotions  
That scrape the ear:  
I praise the brew from Scottish potions,  
In mug or beer.
source_text:
Let other poets raise a fracas
’Bout vines, an’ wines, an’ drucken Bacchus,
An’ crabbit names an’stories wrack us,
An’ grate our lug:
I sing the juice Scotch bear can mak us,
In glass or jug.

text:
Strike the flute—  
its voice has gone still.  
Birds revel  
in sun and starlight,  
nightingale  
in the hollow,  
lark overhead—  
gaily,  
gaily, gaily to greet the turning year.
source_text:
Sound the flute!
Now it’s mute!
Birds delight,
Day and night,
Nightingale,
In the dale,
Lark in sky,—
Merrily,
Merrily, merrily to welcome in the year.

text:
When children’s voices ring across the grass  
and laughter echoes down the sloping hill,  
my heart lies quiet within my chest,  
and all the world falls still.
source_text:
When voices of children are heard on the green,
And laughing is heard on the hill,
My heart is at rest within my breast,
And everything else is still.

text:
Gaze at the climbing sun: there God resides,
Pours forth His radiance, spills His warmth abroad,
And blossoms, woods, wild creatures, humankind
Draw morning solace, noon’s exultant nod.
source_text:
‘Look on the rising sun: there God does live,
And gives His light, and gives His heat away,
And flowers and trees and beasts and men receive
Comfort in morning, joy in the noonday.

text:
I surface through a drench of shivering light,
a blue immersion poured on shadowed stone,
lifting out of the river’s resonant hum.
source_text:
PANTHEA:
I rise as from a bath of sparkling water,
A bath of azure light, among dark rocks,
Out of the stream of sound.

text:
All things hold for voices that are true—  
Where now the soul full-formed, the instrument complete?  
Each utterance from that source arrives with richer, stranger tones,  
unheard on any lesser ground.
source_text:
All waits for the right voices;
Where is the practis’d and perfect organ? where is the develop’d soul?
For I see every word utter’d thence has deeper, sweeter, new sounds,
impossible on less terms.

text:
But why, O death, should we speak thy name?  
Right now we thrive, robust in frame;  
So hoist the canvas, trim the flame,  
Fling Grief to the deep!  
And wide, before Delight’s wild claim,  
Let’s ride the sweep.
source_text:
But why o’ death being a tale?
Just now we’re living sound and hale;
Then top and maintop crowd the sail,
Heave Care o’er-side!
And large, before Enjoyment’s gale,
Let’s tak the tide.

text:
“Alas for Johnie Ged’s Hole this day,”
Said I, “should these dark tidings prove right!
His fine calf-field where daisies lay,
So pale and lovely,
They’ll surely tear it with the plow’s bite;
They’ll wreck poor Johnie!”
source_text:
“Waes me for Johnie Ged’s Hole now,”
Quoth I, “if that thae news be true!
His braw calf-ward whare gowans grew,
Sae white and bonie,
Nae doubt they’ll rive it wi’ the plew;
They’ll ruin Johnie!”

text:
Bending down to the otter’s low-lit pane,  
Brushing the rooftop, staining the barn’s side,  
Tipping her bonnet to the grassy plain—  
And the day’s bright juggler has slipped from sight.
source_text:
Stooping as low as the otter's window,
Touching the roof and tinting the barn,
Kissing her bonnet to the meadow, --
And the juggler of day is gone!

text:
So the Parson could sermonize, sip, and raise a tune,
And we’d be as light as larks in the blush of June;
And prim Mrs. Lurch, forever in her pew,
Would bear no crooked children, no hunger, no stern taboo.
source_text:
Then the Parson might preach, and drink, and sing,
And we’d be as happy as birds in the spring;
And modest Dame Lurch, who is always at church,
Would not have bandy children, nor fasting, nor birch.

text:
Beside her paused the aged nurse in thought,
Till pity pierced her spirit to the root
To see that wretched toil so grimly wrought;
She knelt, her silvered hair around her foot,
And set her withered hands to the foul knot:
Three hours they strove with that unyielding fruit;
At length they touched the grave's deep-hidden stone,
And Isabella neither shrieked nor moaned.
source_text:
That old nurse stood beside her wondering,
Until her heart felt pity to the core
At sight of such a dismal labouring,
And so she kneeled, with her locks all hoar,
And put her lean hands to the horrid thing:
Three hours they labour'd at this travail sore;
At last they felt the kernel of the grave,
And Isabella did not stamp and rave.

text:
My guardian spirit returned once more—
I stood ready, but he found no door.
The wildness of my youth had gone,
and frost now silvered every bone.

There’ll come a time they won’t just scowl—
Some gentle, later hour—
When I don’t pester anymore,
They’ll think how still I stood,
How quietly I whispered “please.”
source_text:
They won't frown always, -- some sweet day
When I forget to tease,
They'll recollect how cold I looked,
And how I just said 'please.'

text:
Who came to give her thanks,
But she lay deep in sleep.
How brief to sail across the sea
And see her likeness, warm with life—
Yet turning home, how long.
source_text:
Who went to thank her;
But she slept.
'T was short to cross the sea
To look upon her like, alive,
But turning back 't was slow.

text:
O who will purchase my infant’s clothes?  
O who will watch me when sorrow flows?  
Who will kiss my cheek where I repose?  
The roving rogue, the father of it.
source_text:
O wha my babie-clouts will buy?
O wha will tent me when I cry?
Wha will kiss me where I lie?
The rantin’ dog, the daddie o’t.

text:
Whoever could reprise a summer’s day  
Would be far larger than his frame, though he  
The smallest speck of humankind might be.  
And one who could retrace the sun,  
Just at the moment it comes undone—  
The slow descent, the bleeding stain I mean—  
When all the East has long been left behind,  
And what is West turns strange and undefined,  
His name would stay.
source_text:
The one that could repeat the summer day
Were greater than itself, though he
Minutest of mankind might be.
And who could reproduce the sun,
At period of going down --
The lingering and the stain, I mean --
When Orient has been outgrown,
And Occident becomes unknown,
His name remain.

text:
My Madeline! soft visionary, darling wife!
Tell me, may I forever be your favored knight?
Your beauty’s guard, heart-formed and crimson-bright?
Oh, silver altar, here I’ll pause from strife,
After long toil and searching, saved to life,
A starving pilgrim—spared by sacred spell.
Though I have found, I’ll plunder not your bower
But take your gentle self alone; if you agree
To trust, dear Madeline, no coarse disloyalty.
source_text:
"My Madeline! sweet dreamer! lovely bride!
Say, may I be for aye thy vassal blest?
Thy beauty's shield, heart-shap'd and vermeil dyed?
Ah, silver shrine, here will I take my rest
After so many hours of toil and quest,
A famish'd pilgrim,--saved by miracle.
Though I have found, I will not rob thy nest
Saving of thy sweet self; if thou think'st well
To trust, fair Madeline, to no rude infidel."

text:
Her mother rose from the daughters of the sea:
The Sun, who gazes on every shore and plain,
Had never witnessed in his endless journey
A form so radiant as she, wrapped within
The amber quiet of her own beauty—
He brushed her with light, and turned to gold
The stone room cradling her repose—
She, in that blissful vision, softly unwove.
source_text:
Her mother was one of the Atlantides:
The all-beholding Sun had ne’er beholden
In his wide voyage o’er continents and seas
So fair a creature, as she lay enfolden
In the warm shadow of her loveliness;—
He kissed her with his beams, and made all golden
The chamber of gray rock in which she lay—
She, in that dream of joy, dissolved away.

text:
She braided the mountain’s wild blooms through her hair,
then, laughing half-mad, wove a wreath in the air,
wet it with tears, leaned over the spring’s bright stare,
and let it fall—gave it up to the wind’s care.
“Go,” she whispered, “when the gale starts to scream,
it’s cruel to drift on a swollen stream,
but I walked from my home like a splintered dream,
my dress is in tatters, and so, they say, is my mind.”
source_text:
Then she wreathed in her hair the wild flowers of the mountain,
And deliriously laughing, a garland entwined,
She bedewed it with tears, then she hung o’er the fountain,
And leaving it, cast it a prey to the wind.
‘Ah! go,’ she exclaimed, ‘when the tempest is yelling,
’Tis unkind to be cast on the sea that is swelling,
But I left, a pitiless outcast, my dwelling,
My garments are torn, so they say is my mind—’

text:
At dusk the swamp-lights, docile as household hounds,
would lap his threaded ankles with cool blue flame;
he’d gaze as they traced their bright bewildering rounds,
a tangle of gestures too lovely to name,
swaying to secret music—until the moon
lifted her first pale eye and they were gone.
source_text:
And the marsh-meteors, like tame beasts, at night
Came licking with blue tongues his veined feet;
And he would watch them, as, like spirits bright,
In many entangled figures quaint and sweet
To some enchanted music they would dance—
Until they vanished at the first moon-glance.

text:
Upon the altar’s height he stood and cried,
“Behold, what demon have we here!” he said:
“One who would make mere reason sit as judge
Over the sacred secrets of our faith.”
source_text:
And standing on the altar high,
‘Lo, what a fiend is here!’ said he:
‘One who sets reason up for judge
Of our most holy mystery.’

text:
You, held in this grave—
from you, the open-handed world,
all earth’s abundance measured out
as vast as earth,
your name a planet: ridged with mountains,
crossed by fields, undone by tides.
source_text:
O thou within this tomb,
From thee such scenes, thou stintless, lavish giver,
Tallying the gifts of earth, large as the earth,
Thy name an earth, with mountains, fields and tides.

text:
They charge the ground and daze the sky,
A crowd of sturdy joy.
Ah! that glares could lie in ambush
For such a foe as this!
source_text:
They storm the earth and stun the air,
A mob of solid bliss.
Alas! that frowns could lie in wait
For such a foe as this!

text:
Spare, O soft zephyrs of morning, the delicate bloom,
As on frost-laden pinions you shepherd the light;
And keep far away, crawling thief of the garden's perfume,
Whose greed strips the lawn of its emerald bright!
Let Bourbon rejoice in his lilies with gilding aglow,
And England in triumph her haughty rose raise:
A lovelier flower decks the valleys below,
Where Devon, fair Devon, in wandering ways.
source_text:
O spare the dear blossom, ye orient breezes,
With chill hoary wing as ye usher the dawn;
And far be thou distant, thou reptile that seizes
The verdure and pride of the garden or lawn!
Let Bourbon exult in his gay gilded lilies,
And England triumphant display her proud rose:
A fairer than either adorns the green valleys,
Where Devon, sweet Devon, meandering flows.

text:
Or, should human strength and pride
Storm the home where you abide,
Through the vaulted heavens you’ll soar,
Mocking all their vaunted power;
On resounding pinions flee
To some far, untainted sea;
And the tyrant you can’t fight,
Spurn in chains—reclaim your flight.
source_text:
Or, if man’s superior might
Dare invade your native right,
On the lofty ether borne,
Man with all his pow’rs you scorn;
Swiftly seek, on clanging wings,
Other lakes and other springs;
And the foe you cannot brave,
Scorn at least to be his slave.

text:
Ah! ah!" she cried, "I never guessed this cruel lot,
I fancied grief alone was sorrow's deepest sting;
I fancied Fortune dealt us joy or not,
Measured our share—bright hours, or death to bring;
Yet here is evil—a brother's blade so hot!
Dear Ghost, thou'st taught my youth its suffering:
I'll seek thee for this truth, and touch thy gaze,
And hail thee dawn and dusk through heaven's ways.
source_text:
"Ha! ha!" said she, "I knew not this hard life,
I thought the worst was simple misery;
I thought some Fate with pleasure or with strife
Portion'd us--happy days, or else to die;
But there is crime--a brother's bloody knife!
Sweet Spirit, thou hast school'd my infancy:
I'll visit thee for this, and kiss thine eyes,
And greet thee morn and even in the skies."

text:
Time of soft haze and honeyed harvest glow,
Dear heart-companion to the aging sun;
You plot together how to overflow
And grace with grapes the vines by thatch-eaves spun;
To weight with apples every mossy bough,
And steep each fruit in sweetness to its soul;
To fatten gourds, and fill the hazel’s hull
With ripened meat; to bid new buds bestow
Still more late blooms to draw the drowsy bees,
Till they believe warm hours will never flee,
For Summer’s drenched their sticky treasuries.
source_text:
Season of mists and mellow fruitfulness,
Close bosom-friend of the maturing sun;
Conspiring with him how to load and bless
With fruit the vines that round the thatch-eves run;
To bend with apples the moss'd cottage-trees,
And fill all fruit with ripeness to the core;
To swell the gourd, and plump the hazel shells
With a sweet kernel; to set budding more,
And still more, later flowers for the bees,
Until they think warm days will never cease,
For Summer has o'er-brimm'd their clammy cells.

text:
Pitcur, that fearless one, tripped in a trench,  
And Clavers caught a sharp, ringing blow, O;  
Else I’d have served a hawk from Athole’s glen  
A feast on the slopes of Killiecrankie, O.  
Had you been there, &c.
source_text:
The bauld Pitcur fell in a furr,
An’ Clavers gat a clankie, O;
Or I had fed an Athole gled,
On the Braes o’ Killiecrankie, O.
An ye had been, &c.

text:
My mother gave me life beneath a southern sky,
My skin is dark, yet oh, my soul burns pale—
Pale as the English child, an angel’s guise,
While I am dark, as though from light exiled.
source_text:
My mother bore me in the southern wild,
And I am black, but O my soul is white!
White as an angel is the English child,
But I am black, as if bereaved of light.

text:
I’ll step out now,
I’ll drift across the country for a spell, though I can’t say where or for how long,
Maybe one morning or deep in the dark as I’m lifting a song, my voice will
quietly cut out.
source_text:
I shall go forth,
I shall traverse the States awhile, but I cannot tell whither or how long,
Perhaps soon some day or night while I am singing my voice will
suddenly cease.

text:
If I could meet you one year from now,  
I’d coil the months like skeins of yarn,  
And store each in a different box,  
Until their turn returns.
source_text:
If I could see you in a year,
I'd wind the months in balls,
And put them each in separate drawers,
Until their time befalls.

text:
I thought the world would not be shaped like this.
I understood there would be cruelty and harm,
Grief and contempt; I never asked to miss
The wounding stones of that steep, narrow track.
In my own chest I saw, as mirrors show,
The hidden rooms of others...So I wrapped my heart,
When I stepped among my kind, in layered calm,
And armed my fragile ribs with quiet brass
To carry scorn, fear, hate—a heavy art.
source_text:
‘Alas! this is not what I thought Life was.
I knew that there were crimes and evil men,
Misery and hate; nor did I hope to pass
Untouched by suffering through the rugged glen.
In mine own heart I saw as in a glass
The hearts of others...And, when
I went among my kind, with triple brass
Of calm endurance my weak breast I armed,
To bear scorn, fear, and hate—a woful mass!’

text:
Does it step with the grace of lilies floating?
Does it wear a plumage like the birds?
Is it carried from distant, storied lands
Whose names I’ve never heard?
source_text:
Has it feet like water-lilies?
Has it feathers like a bird?
Is it brought from famous countries
Of which I have never heard?

text:
Sylvan arrived, a wreath of field blooms tangled
in his hair, scattering as he ran the swollen buds
and drowsy lilies cradled in his arms. We knew
Pan of the hillsides with...
...and spoke:
‘Will you never stop? Love does not keep count.
The pastures with clear brooks, the bees with clover,
the flocks with tender shoots of early spring
are never filled—nor is Love filled with weeping.’
source_text:
And Sylvan, crowned with rustic coronals,
Came shaking in his speed the budding wands
And heavy lilies which he bore: we knew
Pan the Arcadian with....
...and said,
‘Wilt thou not ever cease? Love cares not.
The meadows with fresh streams, the bees with thyme,
The goats with the green leaves of budding spring
Are saturated not—nor Love with tears.’

text:
So they awaited an hour to draw apart
That secret fancy; but their vigil failed,
For rarely she sought the chapel’s heart,
And rarely did a craving make her pale;
And when she stirred, she sped with sudden start,
As a swift bird returns its nest to scale;
And, constant as a nesting dove, she stayed
Beside her Basil, through her tresses wept and prayed.

This land is the widest verse,
Not a country alone but a crowded country of countries,
Here human acts rhyme with the sprawl of sun and the hush of stars,
Here what stirs travels in huge swells, indifferent to the single grain,
Here are the unshaved, the brawlers, the open-handed, the pushback, the soul’s own,
Here the streaming rails, here the press of bodies, the level gaze, the many, the soul’s own.
source_text:
These States are the amplest poem,
Here is not merely a nation but a teeming Nation of nations,
Here the doings of men correspond with the broadcast doings of the
day and night,
Here is what moves in magnificent masses careless of particulars,
Here are the roughs, beards, friendliness, combativeness, the soul loves,
Here the flowing trains, here the crowds, equality, diversity, the
soul loves.

text:
O Isabella, I partly sense
That I may pour my sorrow in your ear;
If you have ever trusted evidence,
Trust now my love for you, trust how severe
My spirit nears its end: I’d not distress
Your hand with unwanted touch, nor would I fear
Your gaze by looking; yet I can’t survive
One more night without confessing my love alive.
source_text:
"O Isabella, I can half perceive
That I may speak my grief into thine ear;
If thou didst ever any thing believe,
Believe how I love thee, believe how near
My soul is to its doom: I would not grieve
Thy hand by unwelcome pressing, would not fear
Thine eyes by gazing; but I cannot live
Another night, and not my passion shrive.

text:
He surely first intended a grand epic, spanning ten cantos,
and the note the printer placed[245:1] before the 1820 collection
might suggest he still held that purpose while composing the
verse. Yet this claim must be wholly dismissed, for Keats,
in his own volume of the works, struck it through and scribbled over,
'I had no hand in this; I was unwell at the time.'
source_text:
It is certain that he originally meant to write an epic in ten books,
and the publisher's remark[245:1] at the beginning of the 1820 volume
would lead us to think that he was in the same mind when he wrote the
poem. This statement, however, must be altogether discounted, as Keats,
in his copy of the poems, crossed it right out and wrote above, 'I had
no part in this; I was ill at the time.'

text:
Hope stands firm;  
Right and Honesty have met their soaring heir.  
“Uprising of Faith.”  
London:  
Edward Moxon, 64, New Bond Street.  
1832.
source_text:
The : Masque of Anarchy. : A Poem. : By Percy Bysshe Shelley. Now first
published, with a Preface : by Leigh Hunt. : Hope is Strong; : Justice
and Truth their winged child have found. : “Revolt of Islam”. : London:
: Edward Moxon, 64, New Bond Street. : 1832.

text:
May Liberty, Accord, and deep Affection,
Join you within the mighty Plan,
Beneath the all-seeing gaze’s protection,
The splendid Builder’s sacred span,
That you may trace the faultless scan,
Ascending by the plumb-line’s call,
Till perfect Radiance fully dawn,
Shall be my plea when distant all.
source_text:
May Freedom, Harmony, and Love,
Unite you in the grand Design,
Beneath th’ Omniscient Eye above,
The glorious Architect Divine,
That you may keep th’ unerring line,
Still rising by the plummet’s law,
Till Order bright completely shine,
Shall be my pray’r when far awa.

text:
It seemed the roads began to flow away,
And then the roads fell mute.
Darkness was all that filled the glass,
And wonder was all we knew.
source_text:
It sounded as if the streets were running,
And then the streets stood still.
Eclipse was all we could see at the window,
And awe was all we could feel.

text:
And Peter Bell, once he had felt  
The lick of newly-kindled flame,  
Grew solemn—by his coat and belt,  
His very stance, the truth was spelt:  
Peter was not the same.
source_text:
And Peter Bell, when he had been
With fresh-imported Hell-fire warmed,
Grew serious—from his dress and mien
’Twas very plainly to be seen
Peter was quite reformed.

text:
Back when the world was young,
No chill of winter stung,
Lovers, radiant, bare,
In sacred sunlit air,
Rejoiced in light that everywhere was flung.
source_text:
In the age of gold,
Free from winter’s cold,
Youth and maiden bright,
To the holy light,
Naked in the sunny beams delight.

text:
A priest stood near and listened to the boy;
With fervent hands he clutched the child’s soft hair,
Then steered him by the collar of his coat,
While everyone admired his pious care.
source_text:
The Priest sat by and heard the child;
In trembling zeal he seized his hair,
He led him by his little coat,
And all admired his priestly care.

text:
Francis, yes—the blade was yours, the sharp
release that carved a tyrant from his guilt,
yours the nerve to meet his blood and smile,
to stand in triumph, spurning all but that;
and yours, fierce glory of your kind, to pry
a despot’s swollen spirit from its throne,
to laugh through sorrow in a calm despair,
to tease life’s sluggish grip with mocking grins,
and rise in joy as griefs around your story spun.
source_text:
Yes, Francis! thine was the dear knife that tore
A tyrant’s heart-strings from his guilty breast,
Thine was the daring at a tyrant’s gore,
To smile in triumph, to contemn the rest;
And thine, loved glory of thy sex! to tear
From its base shrine a despot’s haughty soul,
To laugh at sorrow in secure despair,
To mock, with smiles, life’s lingering control,
And triumph mid the griefs that round thy fate did roll.

text:
Past the professor’s measured words,
past the astronomer’s lens and spectrum, past every proof,
past the surgeon’s theater, the body’s atlas, past the chemist’s glass and formula,
the forms beneath all forms, the eidolons.
source_text:
Beyond thy lectures learn’d professor,
Beyond thy telescope or spectroscope observer keen, beyond all mathematics,
Beyond the doctor’s surgery, anatomy, beyond the chemist with his chemistry,
The entities of entities, eidolons.

text:
Thick with the dust of summer, and a tide  
Of bodies pressing, surging to and fro,  
As numberless as midges in the glow of eventide.
source_text:
Thick strewn with summer dust, and a great stream
Of people there was hurrying to and fro,
Numerous as gnats upon the evening gleam,

text:
Amid the lovely curving braes,  
Where Doon flows, twining, bright;  
Where Bruce once led the battle-frays,  
And hurled his Carrick might;  
A cheerful, kindly, rural band  
Together gathered near,  
To roast their nuts, and strip the land,  
And keep their Halloween  
So glad that night.
source_text:
Amang the bonie winding banks,
Where Doon rins, wimplin, clear;
Where Bruce ance rul’d the martial ranks,
An’ shook his Carrick spear;
Some merry, friendly, countra-folks
Together did convene,
To burn their nits, an’ pou their stocks,
An’ haud their Halloween
Fu’ blythe that night.

text:
The resonant nature of the soil,  
The edge past which thought cannot reach and has no hunger to reach,  
The rightful mother of humankind.
source_text:
The melodious character of the earth,
The finish beyond which philosophy cannot go and does not wish to go,
The justified mother of men.

text:
Whatever’s born to mortal breath  
Must be swallowed back by earth,  
Then rise beyond the reach of time—  
So why should I be bound to you?
source_text:
Whate’er is born of mortal birth
Must be consumèd with the earth,
To rise from generation free:
Then what have I to do with thee?

text:
I often believed that calm had arrived,  
Though calm remained distant and dim—  
Like sailors, wrecked, who think they glimpse the shore  
From the ocean’s deepest rim.
source_text:
I many times thought peace had come,
When peace was far away;
As wrecked men deem they sight the land
At centre of the sea,

text:
Storm heaped on storm, the ocean rearing, climbing,
A steady gale’s bass growl, its low note never breaking,
Bursts of crazed laughter splintering through the dark and fading,
Waves, wind, and the black hour—their fiercest knot tightening,
Out in the blind spray, pale crests galloping, streaming,
Over the slush and sand, ghost-sheets of salt slant stinging,
Where through the thick night the east-born death-wind pushing,
Through whip and blinding wash, watchful and firm advancing—
(That shape far out—a wreck? a flare’s red signal bleeding?)
Slush and sand of the shore, tireless till first light wading,
Steadily, slowly, through the gale’s cracked roar never easing,
Along the black water’s hem by those pale crests streaming,
A knot of dim, strange forms, wrestling the night unbreaking,
That savage knot of three warily watching.
source_text:
Wild, wild the storm, and the sea high running,
Steady the roar of the gale, with incessant undertone muttering,
Shouts of demoniac laughter fitfully piercing and pealing,
Waves, air, midnight, their savagest trinity lashing,
Out in the shadows there milk-white combs careering,
On beachy slush and sand spirts of snow fierce slanting,
Where through the murk the easterly death-wind breasting,
Through cutting swirl and spray watchful and firm advancing,
(That in the distance! is that a wreck? is the red signal flaring?)
Slush and sand of the beach tireless till daylight wending,
Steadily, slowly, through hoarse roar never remitting,
Along the midnight edge by those milk-white combs careering,
A group of dim, weird forms, struggling, the night confronting,
That savage trinity warily watching.

text:
I never understood the generous loaf—
It seemed so far from that familiar scrap
The birds and I would break together, day by day,
At the wild table of the open field.
source_text:
I did not know the ample bread,
'T was so unlike the crumb
The birds and I had often shared
In Nature's dining-room.

text:
In lines two-fifteen through twenty-nine, compare
The Winter’s Tale, act four, scene four, near seventy-three,
Where Perdita bestows on every guest befitting blooms.
Recall as well Ophelia’s garlands in Hamlet, act four,
Scene five, around one seventy-five, and further on.
source_text:
ll. 215-29. Cf. The Winter's Tale, IV. iv. 73, &c., where Perdita
gives to each guest suitable flowers. Cf. also Ophelia's flowers,
Hamlet, IV. v. 175, etc.

text:
“Who owns the small cots,” I said,
“That rest among the dales?”
Some gave a shrug, some grinned instead,
And none unsealed the tales.
source_text:
"Whose are the little beds," I asked,
"Which in the valleys lie?"
Some shook their heads, and others smiled,
And no one made reply.

text:
Her voice fell still.
A single look streamed from her steady gaze across the lifted heads,
That weird tide of captive faces—a thousand shifting, cunning, scarred,
grooved and luminous faces—
She stood, then drifted back along the cramped path threading through them,
Her dress grazing their stillness with a whisper,
And disappeared with her children into the dark.
source_text:
The singer ceas’d,
One glance swept from her clear calm eyes o’er all those upturn’d faces,
Strange sea of prison faces, a thousand varied, crafty, brutal,
seam’d and beauteous faces,
Then rising, passing back along the narrow aisle between them,
While her gown touch’d them rustling in the silence,
She vanish’d with her children in the dusk.

text:
Affection in a shack, on crumbs and rain,
Turns—pardon, Love!—to embers, soot, and pain;
A royal hall's embrace may prove at length
A sharper ache than solitude's spare strength:—
A whispered myth from some enchanted sphere,
Too strange for common hearts to ever hear.
Had Lycius survived to pass his legend on,
He might have bent the lesson toward a frown,
Or sealed it shut: but joy's brief, fragile thread
Prevents the spite and doubt that make soft tones shed.
And there, each dusk, with dreadful, burning stare,
Love, envious of a bond so deeply rare,
Beat wings that roared and whirred in jealous fright
Above the threshold of their room at night,
And flung a lurid gleam along the passage light.
source_text:
Love in a hut, with water and a crust,
Is--Love, forgive us!--cinders, ashes, dust;
Love in a palace is perhaps at last
More grievous torment than a hermit's fast:--
That is a doubtful tale from faery land,
Hard for the non-elect to understand.
Had Lycius liv'd to hand his story down,
He might have given the moral a fresh frown,
Or clench'd it quite: but too short was their bliss
To breed distrust and hate, that make the soft voice hiss.
Besides, there, nightly, with terrific glare
Love, jealous grown of so complete a pair,
Hover'd and buzz'd his wings, with fearful roar,
Above the lintel of their chamber door,
And down the passage cast a glow upon the floor.

text:
O breath of trust, O distant ache  
that seals the exile’s numbered days,  
O every heart worn thin with waiting—  
return now to this hour and mend yourselves anew.
source_text:
O hope and faith!
O aching close of exiled patriots’ lives!
O many a sicken’d heart!
Turn back unto this day and make yourselves afresh.

text:
Oft by the banks of Bonie Doon I strayed,  
To watch the woodbine clasp the rose in shade;  
Each bird poured forth its tale of love anew,  
And I with equal warmth my passion knew.  
With merry heart I plucked a blooming flower,  
So sweet upon its guarded, thorny bower;  
A faithless hand may steal that rose from me,  
Yet, ah, he leaves the piercing thorn with me.
source_text:
Aft hae I rov’d by Bonie Doon,
To see the rose and woodbine twine:
And ilka bird sang o’ its Luve,
And fondly sae did I o’ mine;
Wi’ lightsome heart I pu’d a rose,
Fu’ sweet upon its thorny tree!
And may fause Luver staw my rose,
But ah! he left the thorn wi’ me.

text:
We love to linger by the dead,  
They grow so strangely dear—  
We wrestle with the ones we’ve lost,  
Though all the rest are here.
source_text:
As by the dead we love to sit,
Become so wondrous dear,
As for the lost we grapple,
Though all the rest are here, --

text:
And thus she wasted, thus she passed alone,
Still begging for her Basil to the end.
In Florence, every breast let out a moan
In grief for her dimmed love, without a friend.
A mournful song from this strange tale was grown
And through the land did on all lips depend:
The chorus lingers yet—"O bitter theft,
To rob me of my Basil-pot bereft!"
source_text:
And so she pined, and so she died forlorn,
Imploring for her Basil to the last.
No heart was there in Florence but did mourn
In pity of her love, so overcast.
And a sad ditty of this story born
From mouth to mouth through all the country pass'd:
Still is the burthen sung--"O cruelty,
To steal my Basil-pot away from me!"

text:
How could a bird made for delight  
Perch in a cage and still give song?  
How can a child, when terrors bite,  
Not let his fragile wing hang wrong,  
And lose the spring he’s known so long!
source_text:
How can the bird that is born for joy
Sit in a cage and sing?
How can a child, when fears annoy,
But droop his tender wing,
And forget his youthful spring!

text:
“No one has ever truly known me,” Earth said,
“or told the tale I hold.
You step too far, too bold.”
And scorn broke from her like a lightning thread
as she sang: “Into my breast I enfold
each child when their death knell is tolled,
and so the living stir, by the dead supplied,
and spring leaps up like weeds from those who’ve died.”
source_text:
‘Who has known me of old,’ replied Earth,
‘Or who has my story told?
It is thou who art overbold.’
And the lightning of scorn laughed forth
As she sung, ‘To my bosom I fold
All my sons when their knell is knolled,
And so with living motion all are fed,
And the quick spring like weeds out of the dead.

text:
I won’t recount the entire clash of arms,  
just one brigade sent forward before noon  
to meet the scarlet line—  
how they advanced with even, measured steps,  
how long they held their ground and faced the end.
source_text:
I tell not now the whole of the battle,
But one brigade early in the forenoon order’d forward to engage the
red-coats,
Of that brigade I tell, and how steadily it march’d,
And how long and well it stood confronting death.

text:
Darkness arrived, unlit by moon or star.
Till morning, gathered tribes from distant lands
Encircled that heap, as at a lover’s grave
Two quiet sisters grieve their emptiness;
And in the stillness of that waiting hour,
Above, the serpents’ slither and low hiss
Broke through—so profound—except when plague’s quick power
Struck at long intervals its deadly kiss,
And cut through crowds with screams that traced its path like this.
source_text:
Night came, a starless and a moonless gloom.
Until the dawn, those hosts of many a nation
Stood round that pile, as near one lover’s tomb
Two gentle sisters mourn their desolation;
And in the silence of that expectation,
Was heard on high the reptiles’ hiss and crawl—
It was so deep—save when the devastation
Of the swift pest, with fearful interval,
Marking its path with shrieks, among the crowd would fall.

text:
Mine by the claim of the pale, chosen light!  
Mine by the sovereign stamp!  
Mine by the mark in the crimson cage—  
No iron can clamp!
source_text:
Mine by the right of the white election!
Mine by the royal seal!
Mine by the sign in the scarlet prison
Bars cannot conceal!

text:
Tiny angels who have lost their way,
Plush citizens from Vevay,
Beauties from some vanished summer day,
The bees' own private company.
Paris couldn’t craft the pleat
Belted with an emerald’s heat;
Venice couldn’t show a face
With such a softly gleaming grace.
Never such a hidden snare
As of briar and leaf laid bare
For my little rose-pink dear.
I would rather wear her charm
Than an earl’s distinguished arm;
I would rather live like her
Than be Duke of Exeter.
It’s royalty enough for me
To command the bumblebee!
source_text:
Pigmy seraphs gone astray,
Velvet people from Vevay,
Belles from some lost summer day,
Bees' exclusive coterie.
Paris could not lay the fold
Belted down with emerald;
Venice could not show a cheek
Of a tint so lustrous meek.
Never such an ambuscade
As of brier and leaf displayed
For my little damask maid.
I had rather wear her grace
Than an earl's distinguished face;
I had rather dwell like her
Than be Duke of Exeter
Royalty enough for me
To subdue the bumble-bee!

text:
Held back till all her knowing fled,
Held back till in a gown of snow
Her tender heart lay still.
One hour past the final sigh,
Just one hour after she died—
Oh, stumbling yesterday!
source_text:
Delayed till she had ceased to know,
Delayed till in its vest of snow
Her loving bosom lay.
An hour behind the fleeting breath,
