Later by just an hour than death, --
Oh, lagging yesterday!

text:
Gentle lamb, I'll speak to you;
gentle lamb, I'll speak to you:
You share the name He chose to bear,
for He became a lamb so fair.
He is tender, He is mild,
He came down as a little child.
I'm a child, and you a lamb,
both of us bear His gentle name.
Gentle lamb, may God keep you;
gentle lamb, may God keep you.
source_text:
Little lamb, I’ll tell thee;
Little lamb, I’ll tell thee:
He is callèd by thy name,
For He calls Himself a Lamb.
He is meek, and He is mild,
He became a little child.
I a child, and thou a lamb,
We are callèd by His name.
Little lamb, God bless thee!
Little lamb, God bless thee!

text:
I once held six oxen at the plow,  
And they pulled the furrow steady enow:  
I parted with each, one after one—  
Good ale lifts the heart when day is done!  
O good ale comes, &c.
source_text:
I had sax owsen in a pleugh,
And they drew a’ weel eneugh:
I sell’d them a’ just ane by ane—
Gude ale keeps the heart aboon!
O gude ale comes, &c.

text:
Did he have any wishes at all?
Only his breath, shaped into a sigh,
Would have spoken clearly to me.
And did he stay sure of himself until
Misfortune spilled into an endless deep?
source_text:
And wishes, had he any?
Just his sigh, accented,
Had been legible to me.
And was he confident until
Ill fluttered out in everlasting well?

text:
The world is now the house we live inside—  
wherever the soil still holds a faded sign  
of what was vast and wild, that ground is ours.  
Mild memories of a harsh and hurried kind  
will harvest what our quiet exile sows;  
for who, in some bright corner, free to find  
their own loose thoughts and let them roam the mind  
by water, through the trees, could ever bind  
their face in cynic calm? Child, we will weep.
source_text:
The world is now our dwelling-place;
Where’er the earth one fading trace
Of what was great and free does keep,
That is our home!...
Mild thoughts of man’s ungentle race
Shall our contented exile reap;
For who that in some happy place
His own free thoughts can freely chase
By woods and waves can clothe his face
In cynic smiles? Child! we shall weep.

text:
An eerie voice arose from that wan wraith,  
Its mournful speech contending to express  
The waking tones it once possessed on earth,  
When Isabella clung to its caress:  
A weary falter, and a trembling dearth,  
Like some unstrung harp of a druidess;  
And through it sighed a spectral undertone,  
As rough night winds through tomb-entangled stone.
source_text:
Strange sound it was, when the pale shadow spake;
For there was striving, in its piteous tongue,
To speak as when on earth it was awake,
And Isabella on its music hung:
Languor there was in it, and tremulous shake,
As in a palsied Druid's harp unstrung;
And through it moan'd a ghostly under-song,
Like hoarse night-gusts sepulchral briars among.

text:
Father, father, what road are you taking?
Oh, don’t hurry on so far ahead!
Say something, father, to your small son,
Or I’ll wander off and be gone.
source_text:
‘Father, father, where are you going?
O do not walk so fast!
Speak, father, speak to your little boy,
Or else I shall be lost.’

text:
ASIA:  
Your voice outlasts all sweetness but the source  
From which it flows; still, every love tastes sweet,  
Offered or answered. Love, like light, is shared,  
Its daily sound a thing that never tires.  
As the vast sky, the breath that holds all life,  
It lifts the crawling creature to the star:  
Those who awaken love are truly blessed,  
As I am now; but those who bear its weight  
Through long endurance find a deeper joy,  
As I will soon.
source_text:
ASIA:
Thy words are sweeter than aught else but his
Whose echoes they are; yet all love is sweet,
Given or returned. Common as light is love,
And its familiar voice wearies not ever.
Like the wide heaven, the all-sustaining air,
It makes the reptile equal to the God:
They who inspire it most are fortunate,
As I am now; but those who feel it most
Are happier still, after long sufferings,
As I shall soon become.

text:
PAGE 66, lines 267 onward. These likenesses let us grasp her ordeal
as piercing pain, stirring her out of despair's dull trance,
and granting her a fleeting moment of nearly otherworldly force
and resolve.
source_text:
PAGE 66. ll. 267 seq. These comparisons help us to realize her
experience as sharp anguish, rousing her from the lethargy of despair,
and endowing her for a brief space with almost supernatural energy and
willpower.

text:
Come up from your ancient hollows, you days, till you rise with a harder, wilder grace.
I fed my hungering soul on the raw earth’s gift, long I craved that strenuous taste.
Long I wandered the northern woods, long I stood where Niagara thunders and spills,
I roamed the prairies and lay on their breathing chest, I crossed the high Nevadas, the stony sills,
I climbed the sheer rocks where the Pacific breaks, I pushed my boat into open sea,
I sailed straight into the storm and the storm renewed me,
I watched the rearing mouths of the waves with a fierce delight.
source_text:
Rise O days from your fathomless deeps, till you loftier, fiercer sweep,
Long for my soul hungering gymnastic I devour’d what the earth gave me,
Long I roam’d amid the woods of the north, long I watch’d Niagara pouring,
I travel’d the prairies over and slept on their breast, I cross’d
the Nevadas, I cross’d the plateaus,
I ascended the towering rocks along the Pacific, I sail’d out to sea,
I sail’d through the storm, I was refresh’d by the storm,
I watch’d with joy the threatening maws of the waves,

text:
O Jeany, don’t you lift your chin so high,  
Nor spread your charms for every passerby!  
You scarcely know what wicked, rushing pace  
The storm is keeping:  
Those glances and your fingertips, I fear,  
Have caught its sweeping.
source_text:
O Jeany, dinna toss your head,
An’ set your beauties a’ abread!
Ye little ken what cursed speed
The blastie’s makin:
Thae winks an’ finger-ends, I dread,
Are notice takin.

text:
What is this outburst over goodness and sin?  
Wrong drives me, and the mending of wrong drives me—I remain unmoved,  
My stride is not the stride of a judge or a denier,  
I dampen the roots of everything risen.
source_text:
What blurt is this about virtue and about vice?
Evil propels me and reform of evil propels me, I stand indifferent,
My gait is no fault-finder’s or rejecter’s gait,
I moisten the roots of all that has grown.

text:
I held a star among the heavens,
a single Pleiad, bright and true,
but while my gaze was turned away
it slipped from where it always knew.
And though the sky is thick with lights,
a blaze that floods the dark design,
I cannot bring myself to care—
for none of them is mine.
source_text:
I had a star in heaven;
One Pleiad was its name,
And when I was not heeding
It wandered from the same.
And though the skies are crowded,
And all the night ashine,
I do not care about it,
Since none of them are mine.

text:
What if we, the hollowed and the gone,
were his own skin, his daughters and his spouse—
(1.3.103–105)
For were (104) Rossetti suggests are or wear.
Wear makes a possible mend,
though the line as written holds its ground.
source_text:
What, if we,
The desolate and the dead, were his own flesh,
His children and his wife, etc. (1 3 103-105.)
For were (104) Rossetti cj. are or wear. Wear is a plausible emendation,
but the text as it stands is defensible.

text:
To you, good sir, I dispatch this call,
Please lash till the pony is foaming;
Yet should you inquire of my want,
I candidly reply—nothing.
source_text:
To you, sir, this summons I’ve sent,
Pray, whip till the pownie is freathing;
But if you demand what I want,
I honestly answer you—naething.

text:
Over fortress peaks, where night’s quarreling winds
conscript the storm, and the hollowed valleys listen,
their guardians stand, faces bleached by vigil,
straining toward every rumble in the dark.
source_text:
O’er castled mountains borne, when tempest’s war
Is levied by the night-contending winds,
And the pale dalesmen watch with eager ear;—

text:
Amid the city blocks, a worker’s house emerged,
His long shift over, fresh and tidy, lit by gaslight’s glow,
The rug swept clean, the stove alive with steady warmth.
source_text:
In one, among the city streets a laborer’s home appear’d,
After his day’s work done, cleanly, sweet-air’d, the gaslight burning,
The carpet swept and a fire in the cheerful stove.

text:
I cried through the dark, I cried through the light,
And he brushed every tear from my sight;
I cried through the light, I cried through the dark,
And concealed from him my heart’s secret spark.
source_text:
And I wept both night and day,
And he wiped my tears away;
And I wept both day and night,
And hid from him my heart’s delight.

text:
Sweet Isabel, so innocent and mild!
Lorenzo, Love's young pilgrim, pure and true!
Within one shared abode they could not bide
Without a heart's quick ache, a fevered hue;
They could not break their bread but felt inside
How nearness brought a solace fresh and new;
They could not, surely, rest through night's deep sweep
But dream each other's form, and nightly weep.
source_text:
Fair Isabel, poor simple Isabel!
Lorenzo, a young palmer in Love's eye!
They could not in the self-same mansion dwell
Without some stir of heart, some malady;
They could not sit at meals but feel how well
It soothed each to be the other by;
They could not, sure, beneath the same roof sleep
But to each other dream, and nightly weep.

text:
And there she hushed me into slumber,
And there I dreamed—alas, what grief!
The final dream I ever dreamed
On the chill hill's slope.
source_text:
And there she lulled me asleep,
And there I dream'd, Ah! Woe betide!
The latest dream I ever dreamt
On the cold hill side.

text:
Not the recited markers of ownership shaping my speech,  
but the murmurs of a life unspoken—of release, of disentanglement—  
that dismiss the tamed and the neutral, and lean toward those  
fully alive and unbroken,  
and strike the drum of uprising, and linger with the fleeing,  
with the schemers and the ones who conspire.
source_text:
Less the reminders of properties told my words,
And more the reminders they of life untold, and of freedom and extrication,
And make short account of neuters and geldings, and favor men and
women fully equipt,
And beat the gong of revolt, and stop with fugitives and them that
plot and conspire.

text:
Of bliss—as a skiff, with quick canvas flying  
Down a slow river’s turning, shadowed track,  
Races through black woods where the boughs are sighing...
source_text:
Of rapture—as a boat, with swift sails winging
Its way adown some many-winding river,
Speeds through dark forests o’er the waters swinging...

text:
Gods of the land and the deep
Scoured the wilds for this tree to keep,
But their hunt brought nothing to gain:
One stands in the mind’s domain.
source_text:
The gods of the earth and sea
Sought through nature to find this tree,
But their search was all in vain:
There grows one in the human Brain.

text:
She moved in dance with distant, careless gaze,
Her lips tight-pressed, her breath a hurried stream:
The sacred moment hovered in its place: she sighs
Among the tambourines, amid the teeming dream
Of murmurers in fury, or in jest supreme;
Through glances fond, defiant, bitter, cold,
Enchanted by a fey and phantom gleam;
All dead to earth, save Agnes' lambs unshorn,
And all the joy to wake before the coming morn.
source_text:
She danc'd along with vague, regardless eyes,
Anxious her lips, her breathing quick and short:
The hallow'd hour was near at hand: she sighs
Amid the timbrels, and the throng'd resort
Of whisperers in anger, or in sport;
'Mid looks of love, defiance, hate, and scorn,
Hoodwink'd with faery fancy; all amort,
Save to St. Agnes and her lambs unshorn,
And all the bliss to be before to-morrow morn.

text:
When light has fled, and darkness falls,
And all the world’s at rest;
I dream of him who’s far from here,
Through endless night, and cry, my love,
Through endless night, and cry.
source_text:
When day is gane, and night is come,
And a’ folk bound to sleep;
I think on him that’s far awa,
The lee-lang night, and weep, my dear,
The lee-lang night, and weep.

text:
I asked for little—just enough,
For peace of mind, a glimpse of sky;
Within my means, I’d house such things,
And life and I would walk in step.
source_text:
I meant to have but modest needs,
Such as content, and heaven;
Within my income these could lie,
And life and I keep even.

text:
The swelling gale howled past the craggy steeps,
The hastening clouds raced through the glittering night,
The moaning woods let fall their leafy heaps,
And flashing stars drew quick the frightened sight.
source_text:
Th’ increasing blast roar’d round the beetling rocks,
The clouds swift-wing’d flew o’er the starry sky,
The groaning trees untimely shed their locks,
And shooting meteors caught the startled eye.

text:
Behold this thing, you lords of the hour—
no sum the auctioneer names could ever suffice.
For this, the planet labored in silence quintillions of years
with not one leaf, not one breathing creature,
for this, the faithful cycles rounded and held.
source_text:
Gentlemen look on this wonder,
Whatever the bids of the bidders they cannot be high enough for it,
For it the globe lay preparing quintillions of years without one
animal or plant,
For it the revolving cycles truly and steadily roll’d.

text:
When the conquered enemy begs for calm and rest,
We’ll retreat to the shadows, in passion be blessed.
When the conquered enemy begs for calm and rest,
We’ll retreat to the shadows, in passion be blessed.
O rise and depart, &c.
source_text:
When the vanquish’d foe sues for peace and quiet,
To the shades we’ll go, and in love enjoy it:
When the vanquish’d foe sues for peace and quiet,
To the shades we’ll go, and in love enjoy it.
O mount and go, &c.

text:
All this while, and in every while, the words of true poems stand ready,
The words of true poems are not here simply to delight,
The true poets do not chase beauty but command it, its sober sovereigns;
The scale of the children is the slow, rich breathing-out of the mothers
and fathers,
The words of true poems are the crest and last bravo of knowing.
source_text:
All this time and at all times wait the words of true poems,
The words of true poems do not merely please,
The true poets are not followers of beauty but the august masters of beauty;
The greatness of sons is the exuding of the greatness of mothers
and fathers,
The words of true poems are the tuft and final applause of science.

text:
How often these frail ankles gave way—  
Only the sealed lips know for sure.  
Try—can you loosen that terrible bolt?  
Try—can you shift the iron clasps?
source_text:
How many times these low feet staggered,
Only the soldered mouth can tell;
Try! can you stir the awful rivet?
Try! can you lift the hasps of steel?

text:
When the bright-feathered birds chuckle deep in the boughs,
Where our table is laden with cherries and nuts:
Come dwell, and be joyful, and sing along with me,
The honeyed refrain of ‘Ha ha he!’
source_text:
When the painted birds laugh in the shade,
Where our table with cherries and nuts is spread:
Come live, and be merry, and join with me,
To sing the sweet chorus of ‘Ha ha he!’

text:
The doors of that Chapel stood closed,
And “You must not” was carved on the stone;
So I walked to the Garden of Love
That once with sweet blossoms had grown.
source_text:
And the gates of this Chapel were shut,
And ‘Thou shalt not’ writ over the door;
So I turned to the Garden of Love
That so many sweet flowers bore.

text:
And what muscle, what mastery,
Could knot the fibers of your heart?
And when it first began to pound,
What fierce palm and what fierce stride?
source_text:
And what shoulder and what art
Could twist the sinews of thy heart?
And, when thy heart began to beat,
What dread hand and what dread feet?

text:
Now even kindness, thin and forced, felt strange,
Too scant a gift to hold two lives in place.
They’d sooner fade than stand beneath the gaze
Of law’s hard bondage, and the callous face
It turns to strip a poor soul of its range—
The acid glance, the laughter cold and loud
That men, women, and children wear like a shroud
In these gray rooms where justice turns to ache.
source_text:
And now cold charity’s unwelcome dole
Was insufficient to support the pair;
And they would perish rather than would bear
The law’s stern slavery, and the insolent stare
With which law loves to rend the poor man’s soul—
The bitter scorn, the spirit-sinking noise
Of heartless mirth which women, men, and boys
Wake in this scene of legal misery.
